title: When Outliers are the Goal
concept: In anomaly detection tasks like fraud detection or equipment failure, outliers are the target events we want to predict rather than ignore.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: When is capping (Winsorizing) an outlier preferred over trimming (deleting the row)?

options.text: When the outlier is a valid but extreme measurement, and deleting the row would lose other valuable feature data
When the outlier is a clear system error or typo
When you want to reduce the dataset size to speed up training

exercises.explain: Correct! Capping is ideal when the extreme value is real, allowing you to retain the row's other features while neutralizing the outlier's distortion.

cards.title: A sudden card charge
cards.body: If your credit card is suddenly charged for a purchase in another country, the bank blocks the card. They aren't trying to clean their data; they're looking for that single transaction because it's fraud.

cards.title: Anomaly Detection
cards.body: In anomaly detection, outliers are not noise to be cleaned—they are the target signals. Models are trained to identify these rare observations because they represent significant real-world events.

cards.title: The security guard
cards.body: Imagine a security guard monitoring a feed. 99% of the time, nothing happens. The guard ignores the normal footage and actively looks for the one outlier frame where someone climbs the fence.

cards.title: Critical Anomaly Targets
cards.body: Credit card fraud (0.1% of transactions), factory machine failures (which happen once every few months), and cyberattacks on servers are all examples of outliers that we actively want to predict.

cards.title: Imbalance breaks classifiers
cards.body: Can we use standard classification models here? Usually not! Because outliers are so rare, standard models get 99.9% accuracy just by predicting 'no fraud' every time. We need specialized anomaly detection.

cards.title: Cleaning vs. Detection
cards.body: How we treat outliers differently depending on the project's goal:

left.text:
Outliers are noise
We trim or cap them
Goal: Predict general trends

right.text:
Outliers are signals
We preserve & flag them
Goal: Catch the outliers

exercises.prompt: What is the primary objective of anomaly detection?

options.text: To identify rare and unusual data points
To clean standard data by deleting typos
To make all data points conform to the mean

exercises.explain: Anomaly detection focuses on finding rare, unusual patterns that deviate significantly from standard behavior.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Standard classification models perform exceptionally well on anomaly detection without any modification.
exercises.explain: False! Extreme class imbalance (very few anomalies) forces standard classifiers to ignore anomalies to maximize overall accuracy.

exercises.sentence: In credit card fraud detection, outliers are considered to be the ___ that we want to predict.
exercises.explain: In anomaly detection, the outliers are the valuable 'signal' we want to detect, rather than 'noise' we want to filter out.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a classic application of anomaly detection?

options.text: Predicting when a factory engine is about to fail
Predicting the house price based on square footage

exercises.explain: Engine failures are rare and abnormal events (outliers), making them a textbook use case for anomaly detection.

exercises.prompt: You are designing an email spam filter. Normal emails constitute 99.9% of the traffic, while new phishing emails make up 0.1% and behave very differently from normal emails. If you treat this as a standard cleaning problem and trim outliers, what happens?

options.text: You will delete the phishing emails, leaving the system completely unprotected against them.
Phishing emails will be automatically renamed.
The normal emails will be deleted instead.

exercises.explain: Correct! If you treat anomalies as noise to be cleaned, you remove the very thing you are trying to catch, leaving your system vulnerable. Superb work completing Unit 9 on Outliers & Data Anomalies!
